summary: Soft consonant (ť, ď, ň, ľ) → Masculine or Feminine (check context)
Exception: dieťa (child) ends in -a but is Neuter

rules.title: Determining Noun Gender - Overview
rules.text: Slovak nouns have three genders: masculine (M), feminine (F), and neuter (N). Gender is usually predictable from the noun's ending, though there are exceptions. Knowing gender is essential for proper declension and agreement with adjectives and verbs.

rules.title: Masculine Noun Endings
rules.text: Masculine nouns typically end in consonants. They can be animate (people, animals) or inanimate (objects, concepts).

rows: Hard consonants (p, b, t, d, k, g, ch, h, m, n, r, l, s, z)
muž, dom, stôl, hrad, pes, syn
Soft consonants (j, c, dz, č, dž, š, ž, ť, ď, ň, ľ)

rules.title: Feminine Noun Endings
rules.text: Feminine nouns most commonly end in -a, but can also end in consonants (especially soft consonants).

rows: After soft consonants c, dz, j

rules.title: Neuter Noun Endings
rules.text: Neuter nouns typically end in -o or -e. This is the most predictable gender category.

rules.title: Masculine Nouns Ending in -a
rules.text: Some masculine nouns end in -a, but they refer to male persons or roles. These decline like feminine nouns but take masculine agreement.
examples: Starosta pracuje – The mayor (he) works (masculine verb agreement)
Vidím sudcu – I see the judge (accusative like feminine)
Kolega je tu – The colleague (he) is here
Note: These decline like feminine -a nouns but use masculine adjectives/verbs

rules.title: Foreign Word Patterns
rules.text: Foreign loanwords often follow predictable gender patterns based on their endings.

rules.title: Abstract Nouns in -osť
rules.text: Abstract nouns formed with the suffix -osť are always feminine, regardless of the root word's gender.
examples: Radosť je dôležitá – Joy is important
Múdrosť prichádza s vekom – Wisdom comes with age
Mám starosť – I have a worry

rules.title: Diminutives and Augmentatives
rules.text: Diminutive and augmentative forms follow specific gender patterns.

rules.title: Verbal Nouns (Gerunds)
rules.text: Nouns derived from verbs with the suffix -nie/-tie are always neuter.
examples: Čítanie je zábavné – Reading is fun
Stretnutie bolo dobré – The meeting was good
Cvičenie je zdravé – Exercise is healthy

rules.title: Exceptions and Tricky Cases
rules.text: Some nouns don't follow the standard patterns and must be memorized.
examples: dieťa (child) - ends in -a but neuter, not feminine
starosta (mayor) - ends in -a but masculine (male person)

rules.title: Quick Gender Identification Rules
rules.text: Summary of the most reliable rules for determining gender at a glance.

rows: soft consonant (ť, ď, ň, ľ, etc.)
Check if male → M, female → F

examples: If it ends in -o or -e → almost certainly neuter
If it ends in -osť → always feminine
If it ends in -a → usually feminine (unless it's a male person)
If it ends in consonant → usually masculine
